content:
[S1]: Compound interest is the process where the money you save earns interest, and then that interest itself earns interest over time.  
[READER]: Sounds like basic math, but I’m not sure it really makes a big difference. Prove it’s worth thinking about.  

[S2]: For example, if you save $1,000 at a 5% annual interest rate, after the first year you’ll have $1,050.  
[READER]: Okay, that’s just $50 – not impressive yet. What happens next year?  

[S3]: The second year, you earn 5% not only on your original $1,000 but also on the $50 interest, so your savings grow to $1,102.50.  
[READER]: Alright, that’s a bit more, but it’s still small. How does this keep going?  

[S4]: Over many years, this effect compounds, meaning your money grows faster because you earn interest on an increasingly larger amount.  
[READER]: So it’s like interest on interest? That sounds reasonable, but how much difference does it make really?  

[S5]: After 20 years, that initial $1,000 at 5% interest grows to about $2,653 without any additional deposits.  
[READER]: Doubling and a half in 20 years? That’s better than I thought, but is 20 years realistic for most people?  

[S6]: It is, especially if you start early and leave your money untouched, since each year’s interest adds to the base for the next year.  
[READER]: Starting early sounds like good advice. But what if I add money regularly?  

[S7]: Adding money regularly increases your total savings even faster, as each deposit also earns compound interest over time.  
[READER]: That makes sense. So compound interest works best with patience and consistent saving.  

[S8]: Exactly—while compound interest may seem slow at first, over time it can significantly boost your savings without extra effort.  
[READER]: Alright, that actually sounds like a dependable way to grow money—not magic, but steady and reliable.  

===BEGIN===  
Compound interest is the process where the money you save earns interest, and then that interest itself earns interest over time. For example, if you save $1,000 at a 5% annual interest rate, after the first year you’ll have $1,050. The second year, you earn 5% not only on your original $1,000 but also on the $50 interest, so your savings grow to $1,102.50. Over many years, this effect compounds, meaning your money grows faster because you earn interest on an increasingly larger amount. After 20 years, that initial $1,000 at 5% interest grows to about $2,653 without any additional deposits. It is, especially if you start early and leave your money untouched, since each year’s interest adds to the base for the next year. Adding money regularly increases your total savings even faster, as each deposit also earns compound interest over time. Exactly—while compound interest may seem slow at first, over time it can significantly boost your savings without extra effort.  
===END===
